coredata.dc:title: Self-assembly of graphene quantum dots into hydrogels and cryogels: Dynamic light scattering, UV–Vis spectroscopy and structural investigations
coredata.dc:creator: Allahbakhsh, Ahmad
Bahramian, Ahmad Reza
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the gelation mechanism of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into hydrogels is investigated using dynamic light scattering and ultraviolet-visible techniques. Hydrogels are prepared through the hydrothermal reduction of concentrated GQDs solutions (20–50 mg/mL) using l-ascorbic acid as the reducing agent. At early stages of the gelation process (first 2 h), J-type aggregation of GQDs results in the formation of particles with dimensions around 10 nm. As the gelation process proceeds, formation of larger particles through the aggregation of almost all GQDs results in slower dynamics and hindered Brownian motions in the solution. With the reduction of a majority of edge-carboxyl groups through six first hours of the hydrothermal process, a change in the self-assembly mechanism takes place (from J-type to H-type). A GQDs hydrogel, with an interconnected colloidal morphology and a very low density (~0.009 g/cm3) can be obtained after 8 h of the hydrothermal process. The specific surface area of the prepared GQDs hydrogel, fabricated through the hydrothermal reduction of the 20 mg/mL GQDs precursor solution, is >1000 m2/g. Hydrogels and cryogels prepared using GQDs as the building units can be applicable where hyper-porous structures with large number of available sites are needed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Hydrogel
Self-assembly
Gelation mechanism
Dynamic light scattering

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of GQDs hydrogels and cryogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQDs: (a) TEM image (inset: respective diameter histogram), (b) AFM topographic image (inset: respective height distribution curve), (c) respective AFM phase-change image, (d) FTIR spectrum, (e) XRD pattern, (f) Raman spectrum and (g) formation mechanism of GQDs used in this study as the precursor of the self-assembly process.

DLS results of samples: (a–d) DLS volume-based size distribution graphs of (a) GQDs, (b) 2 h, (c) 4 h and (d) 6 h (insets: intensity-based graphs). (e–h) normalized DLS measured autocorrelation functions (□) and cumulants fit results (dotted lines) of (e) GQDs, (f) 2 h, (g) 4 h and (h) 6 h (insets: respective log-log plots).

UV–Vis and FTIR spectra of samples: (a) UV–Vis spectra of samples (insets: UV–Vis spectra in selected wavelength regions), (b) optical energy gap (Eg) of samples (π-π* transitions), as well as FTIR spectra of (c) 2 h, (d) 4 h, (e) 6 h and (f) freeze-dried 8 h samples.
TEM images of (a–c) 2 h, (d–f) 4 h and (g–i) 6 h samples.
Morphology of GQDs hydrogels and cryogels: FESEM images of (a) outer and (b) inner surface of the freeze-dried 8 h cryogel, as well as (c and d) TEM images of the 8 h hydrogel sample.

Pore structure of GQDs gels: (a) mercury porosimetry cumulative volume (solid lines) and pore size distribution (dotted lines) diagrams and (b) XRD patters of freeze-dried cryogels prepared from 20 and 50 mg/mL precursor solutions. (c) The proposed self-assembly mechanism of GQDs involved J-type and H-type aggregations of GQDs to form larger porous particles with dimensions around 10 nm and the aggregation of these particles to form larger particles and pores.

head.ce:title: Self-assembly of graphene quantum dots into hydrogels and cryogels: Dynamic light scattering, UV–Vis spectroscopy and structural investigations

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Engineering, Faculty of Chemical Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. 14115-114, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the gelation mechanism of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into hydrogels is investigated using dynamic light scattering and ultraviolet-visible techniques. Hydrogels are prepared through the hydrothermal reduction of concentrated GQDs solutions (20–50 mg/mL) using l-ascorbic acid as the reducing agent. At early stages of the gelation process (first 2 h), J-type aggregation of GQDs results in the formation of particles with dimensions around 10 nm. As the gelation process proceeds, formation of larger particles through the aggregation of almost all GQDs results in slower dynamics and hindered Brownian motions in the solution. With the reduction of a majority of edge-carboxyl groups through six first hours of the hydrothermal process, a change in the self-assembly mechanism takes place (from J-type to H-type). A GQDs hydrogel, with an interconnected colloidal morphology and a very low density (~0.009 g/cm3) can be obtained after 8 h of the hydrothermal process. The specific surface area of the prepared GQDs hydrogel, fabricated through the hydrothermal reduction of the 20 mg/mL GQDs precursor solution, is >1000 m2/g. Hydrogels and cryogels prepared using GQDs as the building units can be applicable where hyper-porous structures with large number of available sites are needed.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, graphene-based porous nanostructures have attracted a great attention for many novel applications in different areas such as electrochemical energy storage systems [1,2], catalysts [3], batteries [4], supercapacitors [5], fuel cells [6] and sensors [7]. The main characteristics of three-dimensional graphene assemblies (GAs), which made them promising candidates for such a wide range of potential applications, are high achievable specific surface area, high porosity and large number of available sites [8]. GAs can be fabricated through the one pot self-assembly hydrothermal reduction of a graphene oxide (GO) solution, using an appropriate reducing agent [8–10]. The condition of the reduction process and the characteristics of the GO precursor solution are two parameters affect the final properties of GAs [8] and consequently, the performance of GAs in mentioned application fields.

The quality of the reduction process depends on the condition of the reduction solution [11] and the efficiency of the reducing agent [12]. l-Ascorbic acid (LAA) is a well-known green reducing agent with a high reduction efficiency [8]. Compared to other reducing agents, the main advantage of GO reduction using LAA is the absence of any volatile by-products [8]. Studies on the gelation of GO using LAA demonstrated that the ratio of LAA to GO (LAA:GO) in the precursor solution has a great impact on final properties of GAs [13]. In addition to the concentration of the reducing agent, other important factors such as duration, temperature and pH of the reduction process can also affect the final characteristics of GO self-assembly products [11,12].

Although many factors can be labeled as important in the self-assembly process of GO, but, without any doubts, the size of GO nanosheets in the precursor solution can be considered as the most important factor in this process [12,14]. It is believed that, even though the spectroscopic characteristics and chemical properties of GO nanocolloids are size-independent, but the surface activity and colloidal stability of GO are extremely size-dependent [15]. When the size of GO reaches the nano dimension, the stability of nanosheets improves and the chance of aggregation reduces (due to higher charge density) [15]. Consequently, a reduction in the size of GO into nano dimensions is expected to disfavor the self-assembly of GO. However, as the pore structure of hydrogels is known to be related to the size of building units [12,14], a reduction in the size of GO into the nano dimension is expected to result in an increase in the content of pores and as a result, availability of sites in the structure.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as the most-recently discovered member of the graphene family, have attracted a great attention for many novel applications in different areas such as optoelectronic devices, bioimaging and biosensors [16–24]. GQDs can be described as single (or a-few layered) graphene with the lateral dimension in the size range below 20 nm [25,26]. In this range, quantum confinement and edge effect can be expected in the structure of GQDs [27]. However, graphene fragments with lateral dimensions below ~100 nm are also widely accepted as GQDs [28–30]. Due to hydrophilic edges and hydrophobic central basal planes, GQDs have amphiphilic properties and can be dispersed easily as colloids in an aqueous medium [31]. Coexistence of oxidized and aromatic moieties in the structure of GQDs leads to high possibility of secondary interactions (electrostatic, π–π, hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions) between GQDs, which make them attractive building blocks for the construction of self-assembled architectures [31]. Studies on the self-assembly mechanism of these building units have indicated that the self-assembly of GQDs in dilute and semi-dilute aqueous solutions results in the formation of porous spheres [31,32]. However, these studies are only limited to the dilute and semi-dilute aqueous solutions [31,32] and, to the best of our knowledge, there is no comprehensive study on the self-assembly mechanism of GQDs in a concentrated solution.

Here, we use dynamic light scattering and ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy techniques to investigate the self-assembly of GQDs in concentrated solutions, for the fabrication of hyper-porous hydrogels and respective cryogels. Studies on the gelation mechanism of GO nanosheets through the self-assembly process are mostly focused on the impacts of different hydrothermal conditions on the properties of final products [12,14] and only few works are concerned about mechanisms involved [33]. Here our focus is on the self-assembly mechanism of GQDs into hydrogels. We also investigate the final structural and morphological properties of GQDs hydrogels and cryogels. The self-assembly of a concentrated GQDs solution using LAA as the reducing agent results in the formation of a hyper-porous hydrogel with a high specific surface area, applicable in different areas where porous structures with a large number of available sites are needed.

Citric acid (CA, anhydrous, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, granulated, 99%) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, solution, 37%) were all purchased from Dr. Mojallali chemical company. l-Ascorbic acid (LAA, crystalline powder, 99%) was obtained from Acros Organics.

GQDs solutions were prepared using the hydrothermal method described in the literature [34]. In a typical procedure, 2 g of CA was placed in a 5 mL beaker and carbonized at 200 °C using an electronic oven for about 15 min. Through this time, CA was first liquated into a colorless liquid and then changed into an orange liquid. The orange liquid was added drop-wise (under vigorous stirring) into a beaker containing 100 mL of NaOH solution (10 mg/mL). The pH of the solution after the addition process was 12.26 and the color of the solution was almost dark-red. The solution was then neutralized using an HCl solution (with the addition of 1 mL of the HCl solution (37%) the pH of the GQDs solution reduced to 6). Finally, neutralized GQDs solution was purified using the dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff of 3500).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDs hydrogels and cryogels
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs hydrogels were prepared through the hydrothermal reduction of GQDs solutions (precursor solutions), using LAA as the reducing agent. The optimum ratio of graphene oxide (GO) to LAA (GO:LAA) required for the fabrication of a graphene hydrogel monolith is reported in the literature to be around 1:1 [13]. However, as the sheet size of GQDs is extremely smaller than GO nanosheets, higher amounts of LAA should be required for the successful formation of a hydrogel monolith. Consequently, the ratio of 1:3 (GQDs:LAA) was selected here. Three different precursor concentrations (i.e., 10, 20 and 50 mg/mL) were used to estimate the required concentration of GQDs for the fabrication of GQDs monolith hydrogels through the self-assembly process. In a typical hydrothermal reduction process, 50 mL of the GQDs solution (10, 20 or 50 mg/mL) was mixed with required amounts of LAA (e.g., 7.5 g of LAA for 50 mL of a 50 mg/mL GQDs solution) in a 400 mL beaker. After complete dissolving of LAA in the GQDs solution, the pH of the precursor solution was calculated to be 3.58. The beaker containing the mixture was then placed in a 1 L Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C using an electronic oven. To investigate the characteristics of the GQDs solution through the self-assembly process, the autoclave containing 50 mg/mL GQDs precursor solutions was removed from the oven after 2, 4, 6 and 8 h of heating at 120 °C. The autoclave was then cooled naturally to the room temperature to attain each sample. The pH of the product solutions was measured for each sample right after removing from the cooled autoclave. The products of the hydrothermal self-assembly of 20 and 50 mg/mL GQDs precursor solutions, heated for a complete 8 h process at 120 °C, were a floating thin black layer (GQDs hydrogel), and a dense black layer, formed at the bottom of the reaction beaker (precipitate residue). Hydrogels were separated and immersed in distilled water for one week (solvent changed every day) to remove unreacted GQDs. Hydrogels were then freeze-dried at −80 °C, using a Zirbus VaCo5 laboratory freeze dryer, to prepare cryogels for morphological and structural investigations. Moreover, the precipitate was also washed with distilled water and then dried at 100 °C for further characterizations.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Philips CM30 microscope at the voltage of 150 kV. A Precisa pH 900 pH meter was employed for pH measurements (three repeats for each sample). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained using a Dualscope DS 95-200 DME atomic force microscope. AFM samples were prepared by drying the GQDs solution on a freshly cleaved mica surface. The AFM microscope was equipped with a rectangular cantilever (cantilever coating: aluminum). The AFM microscope was on the non-contact mode, and the scan rate was 5 μm/s. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Perkin-Elmer FTIR spectrometer, according to the ASTM E 1252-98 (resolution: 1 cm−1). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were conducted on a Philips X'PERT MPD diffractometer, equipped with a cobalt anode (wavelength: 1.78897 Å) operated at 40 kV and 40 mA (step size: 0.02°/s). Raman spectra were recorded on an Almega Thermo Nicolet Dispersive Raman spectrometer, equipped with a Nd:YLF laser (532 nm), at the ambient condition (resolution: 4 cm−1, final laser power: 100 mW).

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were carried out using a TESCAN Mira 3 scanning electron microscope at the voltage of 15 kV. Mercury porosimetry results were obtained using a Thermo Finnigan Pascal 440 porosimeter in the pressure range of 0.1–400 MPa and at the ambient temperature. Washburn and buckling theories were used for the calculation of pore diameters, as described in the literature [35]. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were recorded on a Micromeritics TriStar II Plus instrument at 77 K. Brunauer, Emmett and Teller (BET), Barrett, Joyner and Halenda (BJH) and t-plot theories were fitted on the isotherms for the calculation of textural parameters.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS analyzer, equipped with a He-Ne laser (scattering wavelength: λ = 633 nm). All DLS measurements were conducted at a single scattering angle (θ) of 173° and at 25 °C (solvent: water, refractive index of the solvent: n = 1.33). Moreover, zeta potential measurements were also measured using the same analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS analyzer) at 25 °C and θ = 12.8°. All DLS and ζ potential curves and data were obtained using the Zetasizer software (version 7.11) supplied with the analyzer.
Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra (190–1100 nm, resolution: 0.5 nm) were recorded on an OPTIZEN 3220UV double beam UV/Vis spectrophotometer.

As described through the Experimental section, a series of concentrated GQDs solutions were used here as precursors for the fabrication of GQDs hydrogels through a reduction-triggered self-assembly process. Therefore, the structure and chemistry of GQDs can strongly affect the self-assembly process. Typical TEM image of GQDs (Fig. 1 a) demonstrated the formation of quantum dots with almost uniform diameters, disk-like shapes and mostly armchair edge orientation of carbon atoms (edge degree of 120° corresponds to the armchair edge orientation [36]). The mean diameter of fabricated GQDs was around 3 nm (diameter histogram in Fig. 1a), and the thickness of fabricated quantum dots was <1 nm (Fig. 1b). In addition, the FTIR spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 1d) confirmed the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups (required for the hydrothermal self-assembly of GQDs into a hydrogel) on the basal plane (hydroxyl and epoxy groups) and edges (carboxyl groups) of GQDs (see Supporting Information for the IR adsorption table of GQDs). The XRD pattern of GQDs (Fig. 1e) presented a wide 002 peak at around 0.34 nm, which is reported to be a sign for the formation of GQDs through the carbonization of CA (Fig. 1g) [34]. This was completely supported with the Raman spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 1f), where high intensity of the G band (which its intensity reflects the E2g phonon of sp2 carbons and the degree of graphitization [37]) and the low intensity-ratio of D/G bands (ID/IG, represents the degree of defects and disorder carbons in a graphitic structure [37,38]) were observed.

The gelation process of GQDs was investigated using DLS measurements of the initial GQDs precursor solution (labeled as GQDs, 50 mg/mL) and products of 2, 4 and 6 h (labeled as 2 h, 4 h and 6 h, respectively) of the hydrothermal self-assembly process at 120 °C (Fig. 2 ). The size distribution graph of GQDs (Fig. 2a) revealed that the fabricated GQDs had almost a monodisperse size distribution with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.43. As the gelation process initiated, GQDs aggregated to form larger particles and as a result, the size distribution of the 2 h sample (Fig. 2b) was completely polydisperse, with a PDI of 0.77. As reported in the literature [39], this range of PDI may represent the self-assembly of aggregates and therefore, the aggregation of GQDs may initiated at early stages of the reduction-triggered self-assembly process. At this point, particles with size-ranges between 3 and 4 nm and 100–300 nm were distinguishable in the reaction solution (note the intensity and width of the peak-shoulder centered at 3 nm and peak centered at 300 nm in Fig. 2b). This can be related to the simultaneous aggregation of individual GQDs, as well as limited aggregation of larger particles formed through the self-assembly of these individual GQDs.

As the gelation process continued, and after >4 h of the hydrothermal reduction, the peak related to the individual GQDs vanished from the DLS size distribution graphs (Fig. 2c and d), indicating that most of GQDs aggregated through the first 4 h of the process. At this point (Fig. 2c), the volume of particles with sizes around 100 nm increased noticeably. Moreover, larger particles with sizes around 1000 nm formed at this stage of the gelation process. However, as the PDI of the reaction solution at this stage was 0.84, and as this PDI value represents a very broad distribution of particle sizes, one can postulate that the aggregation of GQDs to form nanoparticles and the aggregation of nanoparticles to form larger particles with two size-ranges (i.e., 100 and 1000 nm) took place at this stage of the gelation process.

Considering the unique size distribution of the 6 h sample (Fig. 2d), which comprised of two connected peaks centered at 200 and 500 nm, it can be concluded that the final stage of the gelation process involved the aggregation of large particles at the interface of the reaction solution. Comparing the intensity-based size distributions of 4 h and 6 h samples (Fig. 2c and d), one can observe that the intensity of both peaks related to the particles with dimensions between 100 and 1000 nm reduced in the 6 h sample. It is noteworthy that, visible GQDs hydrogel particles were observable at the surface of the 6 h sample, and there was no sign of any hydrogel particles at the surface of the 4 h sample. The hydrothermal reduction of GQDs for 8 h at 120 °C resulted in the complete formation of a GQDs hydrogel at the interface of the solution. Moreover, the color of the reaction solution changed from yellowish to dark black through the reduction process of GQDs (see Supporting Information for more details).

To investigate the Brownian motions of building units through the self-assembly process, the time-dependency distributions of the autocorrelation function (g1(τ)) of samples were investigated using DLS measurements (Fig. 2e–h). As for the GQDs sample (Fig. 2e), the observed difference between measured g1(τ) data and the ideal exponential decay was probably related to the limited self-assembly of GQDs at room temperature and low pH of the solution (3.58, due to the presence of LAA), which resulted in formation of low-intensity peaks at 30, 500 and 4000 nm in the size distribution graph of the GQDs sample (Fig. 2a). The same aggregation behavior of GQDs through the self-assembly at low pH values has also been reported in the literature [31]. However, as the overall distribution of g1(τ) data was still close to an ideal exponential distribution, and as the complete decay time of g1(τ) was around 1 ms, one can conclude that the gelation at this point was extremely limited, and most of GQDs were in the original individual form.

As the gelation process initiated and after 2 h of the hydrothermal reduction (Fig. 2f), due to the self-assembly of building units, which hindered the Brownian motions of GQDs, the difference between g1(τ) data and an ideal exponential decay increased noticeably. At this point, with the formation of larger particles with slower dynamics (through the aggregation of individual GQDs), the movement of units in the reaction solution hindered noticeably and as a result, the shape of g1(τ) graph differed from an ideal exponential decay distribution. Same results have been reported at early stages of the gelation process of resorcinol–formaldehyde gels in the literature [40,41]. Moreover, since the complete decay of the autocorrelation function of the 2 h sample occurred at times >1 ms (inset in Fig. 2f), it can be concluded that the order of inter-cluster aggregation was very high at this point [40].

With further gelation of the building units through 4 h of the self-assembly process, and with the formation of larger particles with slower dynamics, the complete decay of g1(τ) reached the highest value (Fig. 2g). However, the g1(τ) distribution behavior of the 4 h sample became close to an ideal exponential decay distribution at this point. This behavior can be related to the disappearance of the individual GQDs peak in the size distribution graphs of 4 h and 6 h samples (Fig. 2c and d). As the gelation process progressed for 4 and 6 h, the content of individual GQDs in the reaction solution reduced and the volume percent of aggregated particles (with sizes around 100 and 1000 nm) increased. Consequently, the free Brownian motions of larger particles, without the restriction of individual GQDs, resulted in a decay behavior close to an exponential decay distribution, and a higher complete decay time, for both 4 h and 6 h samples. Same behavior has been reported for resorcinol–formaldehyde gels at final stages (about 70% progress) of the gelation process [41].

In an acidic condition, GQDs start to aggregate when the concentration of GQDs in the solution increases to the concentrations beyond 0.05 mg/mL, as reported in the literature [31]. As the GQDs concentration increases, the zeta (ζ) potential of the system gets more negative and the pH of the solution reduces to lower values [31]. In such a condition, the system overcomes the repulsive forces between GQDs and the self-assembly of GQDs initiates [31]. As in this work the formation of a hydrogel structure is of the interest, the concentration of GQDs in the precursor solution should be high enough to form a three-dimensional self-assembled hydrogel structure. Based on the literature [31], GQDs aggregates can even be formed at concentrations as low as 0.1 mg/mL. However, the size of final particles, form at this concentration, is reported to be under 100 nm (even in the presence of urea [31], which is reported as a strong reducing agent for the gelation of graphene-based materials [42]).

Here, we examined different concentrations of GQDs in the precursor solution for the fabrication of a hydrogel (see Supporting Information for more details). Our results revealed that the critical concentration of GQDs in the precursor solution for the complete formation of a hydrogel structure (under conditions examined in this work) was 20 mg/mL. Higher GQDs concentrations resulted in the increase of the thickness of the hydrogel formed at the interface of the reaction solution.

ζ potential studies (see Supporting Information for ζ potential and pH results) revealed that, as expected, at a high GQDs concentration of 50 mg/mL and low pH of 3.58, the surface of GQDs was strongly charged negatively (ζ GQDs = −50 mV), due to the ionization of carboxyl groups (mainly present at the edges of GQDs [31]). Such a condition (high concentration of GQDs and low pH) is reported to be ideal for the aggregation of GQDs [31], due to insufficient mutual repulsion interactions between GQDs [12]. However, as discussed through DLS studies, the GQDs aggregation was very limited at such a condition, and most of GQDs were in the form of individual quantum dots. The possible reason behind this observation could be highly negative surface of GQDs, which resulted in high mutual repulsive forces between quantum dots. As is known from colloidal science [43], the critical ζ potential for the aggregation of graphene-based materials is expected to be around −30 mV. Systems with a more negative ζ potential are expected to be almost stable, even at pH values as low as 3.5 (note that ζ potential is pH-dependent, as the ionization of carboxylic acid groups is pH-dependent [43]).

As the gelation process initiated, due to the decrease of the pH to 2.41 and consequently, the decrease of surface negativity (ζ 2h = −35 mV), the condition governed the reaction solution favored the aggregation of GQDs. However, as the content of reduced carboxyl groups was not high enough to overcome electrostatic repulsive forces between GQDs, the self-assembly of GQDs did not lead to the formation of a three-dimensional hydrogel structure, and only resulted in the aggregation of quantum dots to form larger particles.

UV–Vis studies (Fig. 3 a) were used to further investigate of the self-assembly of GQDs. The UV–Vis spectrum of the GQDs sample contained two absorption peaks in the UV region. The peak at 253 nm was assigned to the π-π* band, originated from the band to band (π-π*) transitions of sp2 carbons in the structure of GQDs [44]. Moreover, the broad shoulder observed in the range of 310–370 nm could be attributed to n–π* transitions of CO bonds (COOH edge groups) [31]. After 2 h of the hydrothermal reduction, the π-π* band was observed to red-shift to 275 nm, suggesting the restoration of the π-conjugation network of GQDs through the reduction process [43]. Moreover, the disappearance of the n–π* peak could be related to a decrease in the concentration of carboxyl groups [44]. It was expected, as FTIR results (Fig. 3c) confirmed the presence of carboxyl groups in the 2 h sample.

After 4 h of the hydrothermal reduction, as the concentration of carboxyl groups reduced noticeably (Fig. 3d), the surface negativity decreased (ζ 4h = −14.2 mV) and almost all GQDs aggregated to form larger particles. At this point, a new broad shoulder was observed in the UV–Vis studies (inset in Fig. 3a). This transition is suggested to be related to the presence of self-assembled J-type aggregations (with restrained π-π interactions) [32]. As reported in the literature [32], the self-assembly of GQDs can result in two packing molecular arrangements: head-to-tail (J-type aggregation) and pile up (H-type aggregation) arrangements. The formation of J-type aggregations at this point (4 h of the reduction) can be related to the large amount of carboxyl groups at the edges of GQDs. Since the amount of carboxyl groups in the 4 h sample was still high enough to restrain the complete π-π stacking (Fig. 3d), GQDs aggregated through a head-to-tail molecular arrangement (J-type aggregation) [32]. Moreover, the absorption in the whole spectral region increased at this point, which is known to indicate the restoration of the π-conjugation network due to the reduction process [45].

After 6 h of the reduction process, with the elimination of a large number of carboxyl groups (Fig. 3e), a blue-shift at the π-π* transitions to 267 nm was observed, which can be related to the H-type aggregation (parallel molecular arrangement) of GQDs to form larger particles [31]. At this point, conditions required for the complete self-assembly of building units into a hydrogel particle (low pH, almost less negative ζ potential and low concentration of carboxyl groups) almost governed the reaction solution. Therefore, this point can be considered as the starting point of the hydrogel formation (connecting of large particles to form hydrogel particles with micropores). Since even after a complete 8 h of the hydrothermal reduction O-moieties were still observed in the FTIR spectrum of the final product (Fig. 3f), it can be postulated that the hydrothermal reduction of GQDs did not result in the complete elimination of moieties (same phenomenon has been also reported for the reduction of graphene oxide [45]).

Optical band gap (Eg) of each sample can be calculated using the following form of Tauc's equation and UV–Vis results [46]:
(1)
hνα ~
hυ −
E g
n
where α is the absorption coefficient, h is Planck's constant, ν is the frequency of light and n is 2 for an allowed indirect transition or 1/2 for an allowed direct transition [47,48]. As reported in the literature [49], the plot of (αhν)2 versus hν (the photon energy) with n = 1/2 for GQDs aggregates will have a linear region, which extrapolation of the curve tangent in this region to the x-axis will give the Eg value (see Supporting Information for respective plots) [50,51]. The optical band gap of GQDs (Fig. 3b) reduced noticeably through the reduction process, which this behavior can be related to the increase of particle size (through the aggregation process) and the decrease of defects' content (through the elimination of O-moieties) [51,52].

TEM method was employed to further investigate the validity of proposed mechanism for the gelation process of GQDs from a concentrated solution, as shown in Fig. 4 . As discussed through DLS (Fig. 2) and UV–Vis (Fig. 3) studies, the first step of the gelation process (first two-hours of the self-assembly) is proposed to be related to the limited aggregation of GQDs, due to the reduction of carboxyl groups. The limited aggregation of GQDs through first 2 h of the self-assembly process can be observed in Fig. 4a–c. As the gelation process continued, and after 4 h of the hydrothermal reduction, the self-assembly of GQDs through J-type aggregations resulted in the formation of larger particles.

The aggregation of nanoparticles, which resulted in the formation of larger particles, can be observed in Fig. 4d–f (the aggregation of nanoparticles can be observed in Fig. 4e). As discussed through DLS studies, almost all GQDs aggregated after around 6 h of the gelation process and most of aggregations at this step were related to the formation of larger particles (Fig. 4g–i). By comparing Fig. 4e and h, one can postulate that the self-assembly of GQDs nanoparticles in the structure of larger aggregates was completed at final steps of the gelation process (after around 6 h), which this observation is in close accordance with DLS discussions.

FESEM images of the GQDs gel (Fig. 5 a and b), prepared through freeze-drying of a self-assembled hydrogel fabricated through a complete 8 h of the hydrothermal reduction, revealed that the fabricated gel had two different inner (Fig. 5b, the surface formed inside the reaction solution) and outer (Fig. 5a, the surface formed in the contact with the air) surface morphologies. The outer surface comprised from connected colloidal particles with dimensions < 50 nm, which each of them formed through the aggregation of particles with dimensions < 10 nm (inset in Fig. 5a).

The inner surface comprised of large particles with dimensions < 5 μm, which each of these large particles was hyper-porous, and consisted of colloidal particles with dimensions < 50 nm (as also observed in Fig. 4e and f). TEM results also confirmed these investigations, as TEM images of the 8 h hydrogel demonstrated that the GQDs hydrogel formed through the aggregation of particles with dimensions < 10 nm as the building units of the self-assembly process (Fig. 5c and d as well as the inset in Fig. 5d). Moreover, TEM studies (Fig. 5c) also confirmed that the aggregation of particles with different dimensions and the exit of oxygen-containing functional groups from the structure of gel through final steps of the hydrothermal reduction process resulted in the formation of pores with different diameters in the structure of the GQDs hydrogel.

As confirmed by mercury porosimetry results (Fig. 6 a), fabricated GQDs gels had a hyper-porous structure. The pore size distribution of fabricated gels was observed to be depended on the concentration of GQDs in the precursor solution. Consequently, as the pore size in hydrogel structures is known to be dictated by the size of particles, one can postulate that the size of colloidal particles formed through the gelation process increased with the GQDs concentration. Moreover, based on the structural characteristics (see Supporting Information), the specific surface area of fabricated gels also depended on the GQDs concentration, as the surface area of fabricated gels decreased from 1024 m2/g, for the gel obtained from a 20 mg/mL precursor solution, to 547 m2/g, for the gel prepared from a 50 mg/mL precursor solution. However, based on nitrogen adsorption studies (see Supporting Information for N2 adsorption results), the BET surface areas (SBET) of the GQDs gel, prepared from 50 mg/mL precursor solution, was around 702 m2/g (micropore area: 458 m2/g), which is much more than the SBET of self-assembled graphene aerogels fabricated through the hydrothermal reduction of GO using LAA (280–580 m2/g [8]).

As expected, due to the special hyper-porous structure of fabricated GQDs gels (presence of connected colloidal particles with dimensions < 10 nm in the structure of gels in Fig. 5) the density of these gels was in a very low range (~0.009 g/cm3). Moreover, structural studies (Fig. 6b) revealed that an increase in the concentration of GQDs in the precursor solution resulted in a structural shift, from the fully amorphous orientation of GQDs to a slightly orientated one (the appearance of a broad 002 peak in the XRD pattern of the 20 mg/mL sample). This phenomenon can be related to the increase of particle size with the concentration of GQDs in the precursor solution, as larger particles at a higher GQDs concentration were observed to be products of the H-type aggregation of GQDs (through the π-π stacking) and consequently, more orientation in the self-assembled structure (Fig. 6c).

In summary, we investigated the self-assembly of GQDs using DLS, UV–Vis spectroscopy and structural studies, by tracing structural changes at different reduction stages of the hydrothermal process. DLS studies confirmed that most of GQDs aggregated through the first 4 h of the reduction process. At the final stages (between 4 and 8 h of the reduction), most of aggregations were related to the aggregation of GQDs to form larger particles, and also aggregations between larger particles. Based on our results, 8 h of the hydrothermal reduction of a GQDs precursor solution with concentrations larger than 20 mg/mL is enough for the formation of a self-assembled hydrogel. Higher concentrations of GQDs can lead to the formation of larger building units and consequently, larger pores in the structure of final hydrogels. UV–Vis studies confirmed that the reduction of GQDs resulted in the elimination of edge carboxyl groups. With the elimination of edge-carboxyl groups, the mechanism of the self-assembly process changed from J-type to the H-type. Moreover, morphological and structural studies revealed that the aggregation of connected colloidal particles with different particle sizes resulted in the formation of pores with a wide range of pore size distribution. As the GQDs concentration in the reaction solution increased, the size of pores in the structure of the final gel increased. Based on our results, the structure of GQDs hydrogels and cryogels can be engineered by controlling the conditions of the self-assembly process, to reach a hyper-porous structure with an extremely high specific surface area and lots of available sites for different applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Tarbiat Modares University and Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council (INIC) for supporting this research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
FTIR peak assignments of GQDs, complete formation procedure of the GQDs hydrogel (8 h) from citric acid (CA), FTIR spectra of 8 h gelation precipitate and LAA, and the XRD pattern of 8 h gelation precipitate, Zeta (ζ) potential and pH results of samples, details on the role of GQDs concentration on the gelation process, structural characteristics of GQDs cryogels, plots of
ε
/ λ versus 1/λ for the calculation of optical band gaps according to Eq. (1), N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore-size distribution curve of GQDs cryogels. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2018.05.123.
